Farn Polrrrcal PRACTTcES CouvrrssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95g14_2329
(916) 322-s660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
December 5,2006
Jim Wood
432 Greens Drive
Healdsburg, CA 95448
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-06-216
Dear Mr. Wood
This letter is-in response to your request for advice regarding the honoraria
provisions of the political Reform Act (the ,.Act,,).r
' Go,r.,rment Code sections g I 000_-9 1014
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations
QUESTION
CONCLUSION
FACTS
Commission regulations appear at title 2. sectiols
would payments for your lectures or presentations to professional groups on the
subject of forensic dentistry be "honoraria,, piohibit"J tf1t " a"tz
Under the circumstances you describe, these payments would not be classified as
prohibited "honoraria," but as "earned income,, undei the act. Those payments rvoulrl
therefore be reportabre as income, but such incom" r, not frohibited by the Act.
You are a recently-elected member of the Healdsburg city council. you are also
a full-time practicing dentist with an office in cloverdale, and, arecognized expeft in the
field of forensic dentistry. You consult for the Mendocino, Luk", urrj S;;;;; County
Sheriff s Departments on cases involving forensic dentisiry. you are sometimes invitecl
to speak to dentai organizations on the topic of forensic dentistry, generally receiving
some payment for your time and effort. You have never been inviied to make a paid
appearance before a dentar group within the city limits of Heardsburg, but you speak
occasionally to civic groups without remuneration.
.at@


File No. A-06-216
Page No. 2
ANALYSIS
The Act's prohibition on acceptance of an "honorarium" appears at section
895A2@), which states in full:
"No elected state officer, elected officer of a local government agency, or
other individual specified in Section 87200 shall accept any honor aium.,,
As a member of the Healdsburg city council, you are "an elected officer of a local
government agency" within the meaning of sections 89502(a) and, B2O41, and as such are
subject to the Act's prohibition on accepting honoraria. The honorarium ban also applies
to candidates fot local elected office. (See sections 82020,89502(bXl).) yourposition
makes you subject to this ban, so we must next consider whether pay*errts for the
activities you describe would be considered "honoraria" as this te'rm is used by the Act.
Section 89501(a) defines the term "honorarium," in pertin entparl, as follows:
"(a) Forpurpofes of this chapter, 'honorarium'means, except as
provided in subdivision (b), any payment made in consideration for
any speech given, article published, or attendan ce at any public or
private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering.
(b) The term 'honorarium, does not include:
(1) Earned income for personal services which are customarily
provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide business,
trade, or profession, such as teaching, practicing law, medicine,
insurance, real estate, banking, or building contiacting, unless the
sole or predominant activity of the business, trade, or profession is
making speeches. The commission shall adopt regulations to
implement this subdivision.,,
Regulation 18931.1 also provides, in pertinent part, that: .,For purposes of
Government Code Sections 89501 through 89506, 'speech given' ,rr"*, a public address,
oration, or other form of oral presentation." This definition of the.'speech given,,for
purposes of the honorarium ban at section 89502 is broad enough to includJthe paid
lectures you describe, unless they are made in the normal course of practice in a bona fide
business, trade or profession.
Over the years we have discussed "bona fide business, trade, or profession,, in
advice letters describing a wide variety of paid activities. In the Tribe Advice Letter,
No' A-95-3 10, we concluded that the Deputy Executive Director of this agency would


File No. A-06-216
Page No. 3
not be receiving a prohibited honorarium if he accepted paid employment as a radio talk
show host. Similarly, in the Leach Advice Letter,No. A-97-l 60, andin the KahnAdvice
Lettet, No. 4-99-296,we found that payments given for instructional presentations were
not honoraria, but were "earned income" for services rendered in a bona fide business,
trade, orprofession, under section 89501(b).
In these cases, the officials had engaged in the same business, trade or profession
before he or she entered public service. Both the Leach and Kahn letters concerned
business activities of Assembly candidates who had been engaged for many years in a
business unrelated to the duties or expertise of Assembly members. As we understand
the circumstances here, your situation is similar to those described above, differing only
in that, as a dentist, you practice a state-licensed profession which is presumed - in the
absence of evidence to the contrary - to be a"bona fide" profession under Regulation
1 893 2. 1 (c)( I ), whi ch provides in pertinen t p art that:
"(c) For purposes of Government Code Sections g9501
through 89506, atrade or professional practice is presumed
to be "bonafrde" if either of the following applies:
(1) The tradesperson or professional practitioner possesses
current licensure or certification which is issued by state or
federal occupational licensing authority, the issuance of
which requires a demonstration of skill and knowledge in
connection with the relevant trade or profession.,'
Subdivision (d) of the same regulation adds this proviso: "A business whose
predominant activity is making speeches is not "bona fide" for pulposes of Government
Code Sections 89501 through 89506." Your particular circumstances are most similar to
those we discussed in the Phillips Advice Letter, No. A-91-137, where we applied the
"earned income" exception to lectures given by a physician to health "ur" p-f"rsionals
on areas of medicine in which the physician practiced and conducted research.
As we understand the facts, you are a licensed dentist with a full-time family
practice, who in addition consults with law enforcement agencies in cases involving
forensic dentistry. It appears then that your predominant *tiuiti"s are clinical dentistry,
with some forensic work in addition, and that your presentations on forensic dentistry are
merely anclllary to a secondary component of your professional practice. Assuming that


File No. A-06-216
Page No. 4
this is an accurate assessment of your activities, we have little difficulty in concluding
that the payments you receive in compensation for your presentations on forensic
dentistry are "eamed income" and not "honoraria" as these terms are defined by the Act.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
?hrr^'o{Wk
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
LTwjgl

sections.question_synthetic: Whether payments received by a newly-elected city council member who is a practicing dentist for lectures and presentations on forensic dentistry to professional groups would constitute prohibited 'honoraria' under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The payments would not be classified as prohibited 'honoraria' but rather as 'earned income' under the Political Reform Act, and are therefore reportable as income but not prohibited.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; Skipped (too short): conclusion, question; QUESTION has only 0 words; CONCLUSION has only 0 words; LLM: Document has minimal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections (marked as 0 words); however, substantive question and conclusion are present in the document body. Facts, Analysis, and conclusion provided in letter body. Document is a complete advice letter with clear legal determination.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether payments received by a newly-elected city council member who is a practicing dentist for lectures and presentations on forensic dentistry to professional groups would constitute prohibited 'honoraria' under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The payments would not be classified as prohibited 'honoraria' but rather as 'earned income' under the Political Reform Act, and are therefore reportable as income but not prohibited.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that a Healdsburg city council member's payments for lectures on forensic dentistry to professional groups constitute earned income rather than prohibited honoraria, as these presentations are ancillary to the official's bona fide dental practice.
